name: Pinot Noir
primaryAromasNote: Red fruit profile: strawberry, raspberry, red cherry.
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age

styleByRegion.burgundy: Classic home of Pinot Noir. Cool to moderate climate gives high-acid, light- to medium-bodied wines with red fruit (strawberry, raspberry, red cherry); careful use of oak. Hierarchy of GIs: regional Bourgogne AOC, village wines (e.g. Gevrey-Chambertin), Premier Cru and Grand Cru top sites. Outstanding ageing potential at the top levels.
styleByRegion.gevrey-chambertin: Côte d'Or village in the Côte de Nuits (northern half). Premium Pinot Noir — fuller-bodied for Pinot, structured, developing earthy/forest-floor characters with age. Outstanding ageing potential. Site of multiple Grand Cru vineyards including Le Chambertin.
styleByRegion.nuits-saint-georges: Côte d'Or village in the Côte de Nuits. Premium Pinot Noir; structured, with red fruit and earthy/forest-floor characters with age.
styleByRegion.beaune: Côte d'Or village in the Côte de Beaune (southern half). Premium Pinot Noir; medium-bodied, fragrant, with red fruit.
styleByRegion.pommard: Côte d'Or village in the Côte de Beaune. Premium Pinot Noir; tends to be more structured and tannic than its Côte de Beaune neighbours.
styleByRegion.sonoma: California region cooler than Napa thanks to Pacific influence. Premium Pinot Noir from cool sub-areas; medium-bodied with red fruit and oak influence.
styleByRegion.carneros: Cool sub-AVA spanning the southern ends of Napa and Sonoma counties; cooled by San Pablo Bay fog. Premium Pinot Noir with high acidity and red fruit; widely used as base for sparkling wine.
styleByRegion.santa-barbara-county: Cool, Pacific-influenced county on California's central coast. Premium Pinot Noir with high acidity, red fruit, and judicious oak.
styleByRegion.oregon: Cool-climate Pacific Northwest state. The signature US region for premium Pinot Noir — high acidity, red fruit (strawberry, red cherry), elegant style with careful oak.
styleByRegion.casablanca-valley: Cool coastal Chilean valley near Santiago, Pacific fog-cooled. Premium Pinot Noir with high acidity and red fruit.
styleByRegion.walker-bay: Cool coastal South African region (Hemel-en-Aarde Valley). Atlantic-influenced; premium Pinot Noir with high acidity and red fruit.
styleByRegion.yarra-valley: Victoria. Cool to moderate climate. Premium Pinot Noir with high acidity and red fruit; oak ageing common.
styleByRegion.mornington-peninsula: Victoria, just south of Melbourne. Cool maritime climate. Premium Pinot Noir with high acidity and red fruit; elegant, fragrant style.
styleByRegion.martinborough: Wairarapa, North Island NZ. Cool to moderate climate. Premium Pinot Noir with high acidity and red fruit.
styleByRegion.marlborough: South Island NZ. Cool, sunny climate. Pinot Noir at high volume — medium-bodied with red fruit. Better known for Sauvignon Blanc.
styleByRegion.central-otago: South Island NZ — the world's southernmost wine region. Cool, dry continental climate (rare in NZ). Premium Pinot Noir of outstanding quality with concentrated red and dark cherry fruit.

name: Zinfandel
primaryAromasNote: Red fruit (strawberry, raspberry) and black fruit (plum, black cherry).
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age
winemaking: White Zinfandel is a medium-sweet rosé style

styleByRegion.california: USA. Warm climate gives ripe, full-bodied red Zinfandel with high alcohol, intense black fruit, and oak-derived vanilla and coffee notes; can develop dried fruit/raisin and earth/meat with age. White Zinfandel is a medium-sweet rosé style — high volume.
styleByRegion.puglia: Southern Italy ('the heel'). Hot Mediterranean climate. Same grape as Zinfandel, locally called Primitivo. Soft, fruity, dry, full-bodied reds; often less alcoholic than California Zin.

name: Riesling
primaryAromasNote: Aromatic variety. Fruit characteristics vary according to ripeness — cool climates show green apple, lemon and floral notes; warmer or riper styles give stone fruit, tropical fruit, and (when extra-ripe or botrytised) dried apricot. Susceptible to botrytis/noble rot.
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age — develops honey and petrol (gasoline) characters with bottle age.

styleByRegion.alsace: Alsace, France. Bordering Germany; cool climate (rain shadow of the Vosges keeps it dry and sunny). Riesling here is typically dry, full-bodied, intensely aromatic with citrus, stone fruit and floral notes; high acidity. Top sites are designated Alsace Grand Cru.
styleByRegion.mosel: Northernmost German region; steep slopes above the Mosel river. Cool climate. Riesling here is typically light-bodied with low alcohol and high acidity, often off-dry to medium-sweet (Kabinett, Spätlese), with delicate green apple, lemon and floral character; outstanding ageing potential.
styleByRegion.rheingau: South-facing slopes along the Rhine. Slightly warmer than the Mosel. Premium Riesling — typically more body and ripeness than Mosel; often dry (Trocken) or off-dry, with citrus, stone fruit and honey character; outstanding ageing potential.
styleByRegion.pfalz: Warmer German region south of the Rheingau. Riesling here is fuller-bodied and riper, often dry, with stone fruit and tropical fruit notes.
styleByRegion.clare-valley: South Australia at high altitude. Cool nights despite warm days. Premium dry Riesling with high acidity, intense citrus (lime) and floral notes; ages outstandingly with petrol/honey development.
styleByRegion.eden-valley: South Australia, near the Barossa, at high altitude. Cool to moderate. Premium dry Riesling with high acidity and citrus/floral notes; ages outstandingly.

name: Chenin Blanc
primaryAromasNote: Just-ripe Chenin shows green fruit (apple) and citrus (lemon); extra-ripe gives stone fruit (peach) and tropical fruit (pineapple, mango). Susceptible to botrytis/noble rot, used to make sweet styles.
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age

styleByRegion.vouvray: Loire Valley AOC, central Loire. Cool climate. The reference for Loire Chenin Blanc — full range of styles from dry through off-dry and medium-sweet to sweet (botrytis-affected). Typically unoaked. High acidity and apple/lemon character; outstanding examples can age, developing honey notes.
styleByRegion.western-cape: South Africa's broad GI. South Africa is the largest Chenin Blanc producer in the world. Styles range from light, dry, fruit-forward whites to richer, oak-aged, complex examples (often from old vines).

name: Furmint
primaryAromasNote: Green fruit and stone fruit. Susceptible to botrytis/noble rot, used for sweet styles (Tokaji Aszú).
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age

styleByRegion.tokaj: Hungary's signature wine region. Moderate climate; autumn mists encourage botrytis/noble rot. Range of styles from dry through off-dry and medium to sweet. The famous Tokaji Aszú is a sweet wine from botrytis-affected grapes — concentrated, sweet, with dried fruit, caramel and honey notes; outstanding ageing potential.

name: Sémillon
primaryAromasNote: Youthful Sémillon shows grass, green apple and lemon. Susceptible to botrytis/noble rot, which produces concentrated, sweet wines. Many harvest options give styles ranging from dry to sweet.
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age
blendingNotes: Often blended with Sauvignon Blanc in Bordeaux for both dry whites and sweet Sauternes. Sémillon contributes body, weight and ageing capacity to the blend.

styleByRegion.bordeaux: Used in Bordeaux's dry whites at the regional Bordeaux AOC level (and at premium level in Graves and Pessac-Léognan), blended with Sauvignon Blanc. Sémillon contributes body, weight and ageing capacity.
styleByRegion.sauternes: Top sweet wine appellation south of the city of Bordeaux. Sémillon-dominant blend with Sauvignon Blanc, from grapes affected by botrytis/noble rot — morning mists from the Ciron river meeting the warmer Garonne create the necessary conditions. Concentrated, sweet, full-bodied with honey, marmalade and dried-fruit notes; outstanding ageing potential.
styleByRegion.hunter-valley: New South Wales. Famous for distinctive long-lived dry Sémillon — picked early at low alcohol, unoaked, with high acidity and citrus/green apple in youth, developing toast, honey and hazelnut with extended bottle age.
styleByRegion.barossa-valley: South Australia. Sémillon here produces fuller-bodied, riper styles than Hunter; can be oaked.

name: Chardonnay
primaryAromasNote: Versatile across cool, moderate and warm climates. Cool climates give green apple and lemon; moderate climates give stone fruit (peach); warm climates give tropical fruit (banana, pineapple).
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age
winemaking: Malolactic conversion — adds butter and cream
Lees contact — adds body and biscuit/bread notes
Oak (new vs old, level of toast, size of vessel)

styleByRegion.south-of-france-pgi: High-volume varietal Chardonnay from Pays d'Oc IGP. Warm climate gives ripe, fruit-forward styles with stone fruit and tropical fruit; oak commonly used at this level.
styleByRegion.burgundy: Classic home of Chardonnay. Cool to moderate climate; styles range from unoaked, citrus-driven Chablis (north) to richer, full-bodied, oak-aged whites of the Côte d'Or and Mâconnais (south).
styleByRegion.chablis: Northern, coolest part of Burgundy. Cool climate gives high acidity and green apple/lemon/citrus notes; typically unoaked or only lightly oaked. Premier Cru and Grand Cru classifications mark top sites.
styleByRegion.puligny-montrachet: Côte d'Or commune (Côte de Beaune). Premium full-bodied Chardonnay; oak ageing with malolactic conversion and lees contact gives butter, cream and biscuit notes alongside ripe stone fruit. Outstanding ageing potential.
styleByRegion.meursault: Côte d'Or commune (Côte de Beaune). Premium full-bodied Chardonnay; oak-aged with butter and cream from malolactic conversion and lees contact, plus stone fruit and hazelnut characters.
styleByRegion.macon: Mâconnais sub-region (southern Burgundy). Warmer than the Côte d'Or; ripe, fruity, medium-bodied Chardonnay, often unoaked or lightly oaked, at accessible prices.
styleByRegion.pouilly-fuisse: Top Mâconnais commune. Premium Chardonnay with more body and concentration than basic Mâcon; often oak-aged.
styleByRegion.california: State-wide California GI. Warm climate gives ripe, full-bodied, fruit-forward Chardonnay; oak ageing and malolactic conversion common, producing tropical fruit, vanilla and butter notes.
styleByRegion.sonoma: Cooler than Napa thanks to Pacific influence. Range of styles from cool-climate citrus and stone fruit to richer oak-aged styles.
styleByRegion.carneros: Cool sub-AVA spanning the southern ends of Napa and Sonoma, cooled by San Pablo Bay fog. Premium Chardonnay (and Pinot Noir) with high acidity and citrus/stone fruit; widely used for sparkling wine base too.
styleByRegion.napa-valley: Warm climate gives ripe, full-bodied, oak-aged Chardonnay with tropical fruit and vanilla; very good to outstanding examples.
styleByRegion.santa-barbara-county: Cool, Pacific-influenced county on California's central coast. Premium Chardonnay with high acidity and citrus/stone fruit; oak-aged styles common.
styleByRegion.oregon: Cool-climate Pacific Northwest state. Premium Chardonnay with high acidity and citrus/apple/stone fruit; some oak influence.
styleByRegion.central-valley-chile: Chile's largest production zone. Warm climate gives ripe, fruit-forward Chardonnay at high volume; good value.
styleByRegion.casablanca-valley: Cool coastal valley near Santiago, cooled by Pacific fogs. Premium Chardonnay with high acidity and citrus/stone fruit.
styleByRegion.western-cape: Broad GI covering most of South Africa's wine regions. Warm climate gives ripe, fruit-forward Chardonnay across the price spectrum; oak ageing common.
styleByRegion.walker-bay: Cool coastal South African region (Hemel-en-Aarde Valley). Atlantic-influenced; premium Chardonnay (and Pinot Noir) with high acidity and citrus/stone fruit.
styleByRegion.south-eastern-australia: Multi-state catch-all GI. Used for high-volume varietal Chardonnay blended from across regions; ripe, soft, oak-flavoured.
styleByRegion.yarra-valley: Victoria. Cool to moderate climate gives premium Chardonnay with high acidity and citrus/stone fruit; oak ageing common.
styleByRegion.adelaide-hills: South Australia. Cool to moderate climate at high altitude. Premium Chardonnay with high acidity and citrus/stone fruit; oak ageing common.
styleByRegion.margaret-river: Western Australia. Maritime climate gives premium Chardonnay with stone and tropical fruit; oak ageing and lees contact common.
styleByRegion.marlborough: South Island, NZ. Cool, sunny climate gives premium Chardonnay with high acidity and citrus/stone fruit; some oak ageing. Better known for Sauvignon Blanc.
styleByRegion.hawkes-bay: North Island, NZ — warmer than Marlborough. Premium Chardonnay with stone fruit and tropical fruit; oak ageing common.

name: Sauvignon Blanc
primaryAromasNote: Aromatic variety with herbaceous notes (grass, asparagus, green pepper) plus fruit ranging from green and citrus (cooler) through stone fruit to tropical (warmer).
winemaking: Typically single varietal (sometimes blended with Sémillon)

styleByRegion.south-of-france-pgi: High-volume varietal Sauvignon Blanc from Pays d'Oc IGP. Warm climate gives ripe, fruit-forward, soft styles for everyday drinking.
styleByRegion.loire: Cool to moderate northern French river valley. Sauvignon Blanc is the signature white grape of the eastern Loire (Sancerre, Pouilly-Fumé) and central Loire (Touraine).
styleByRegion.sancerre: Top Loire Sauvignon Blanc AOC, on the eastern bank of the Loire. Cool climate gives high acidity, citrus, stone fruit and herbaceous/grassy notes, sometimes with a flinty mineral character.
styleByRegion.pouilly-fume: Loire Sauvignon Blanc AOC opposite Sancerre on the western bank. Similar style to Sancerre, often noted for a smoky/flinty character (the 'fumé' in the name).
styleByRegion.touraine: Central Loire AOC. Lighter, more affordable Sauvignon Blanc with high acidity, citrus and herbaceous notes.
styleByRegion.bordeaux: Sauvignon Blanc is used in Bordeaux's dry whites at the regional Bordeaux AOC level, often blended with Sémillon.
styleByRegion.graves: Left Bank south of the city of Bordeaux. Sauvignon Blanc-Sémillon dry white blends; medium- to full-bodied, often oak-aged.
styleByRegion.pessac-leognan: Top sub-zone of Graves. Premium dry whites from Sauvignon Blanc and Sémillon, oak-aged with lees contact for complexity; outstanding examples can age.
styleByRegion.napa-valley: Warm climate gives ripe, fruit-forward Sauvignon Blanc with stone fruit and tropical notes; sometimes oak-aged in a 'Fumé Blanc' style.
styleByRegion.central-valley-chile: Warm climate gives ripe, fruit-forward Sauvignon Blanc at high volume; good value.
styleByRegion.casablanca-valley: Cool coastal Chilean valley, Pacific fog-cooled. Premium Sauvignon Blanc with high acidity and citrus, stone fruit and tropical notes — closer in style to Marlborough than to warm Central Valley.
styleByRegion.elgin: Cool South African region at high altitude in the Cape. Premium Sauvignon Blanc with high acidity and citrus/herbaceous character.
styleByRegion.constantia: Cool South African region near Cape Town, cooled by South Atlantic. Premium Sauvignon Blanc with citrus, herbaceous and tropical notes.
styleByRegion.adelaide-hills: South Australia at high altitude (cool to moderate). Premium Sauvignon Blanc with high acidity and citrus/stone fruit/tropical notes.
styleByRegion.margaret-river: Western Australia. Maritime climate. Sauvignon Blanc often blended with Sémillon (Bordeaux-style white blend).
styleByRegion.marlborough: South Island NZ. Cool, sunny climate. The benchmark New World Sauvignon Blanc — pungent and herbaceous (asparagus, grass, green pepper) with tropical notes (passion fruit) and high acidity. Typically unoaked, single varietal, for early drinking.

name: Pinot Grigio
primaryAromasNote: Two key styles produced. Light Italian style ('Pinot Grigio'): dry, medium to high acidity, light-bodied, simple — green fruit (pear) and citrus (lemon). Richer Alsatian style ('Pinot Gris'): dry, off-dry or medium, medium acidity, full-bodied, complex — stone fruit (peach) and tropical fruit (banana, mango).
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age
winemaking: Italian style: dry, medium to high acidity, light-bodied, simple
Alsatian style: dry / off-dry / medium, medium acidity, full-bodied, complex

styleByRegion.veneto: Italy IGT/IGP. High-volume Pinot Grigio in the Italian style — dry, light-bodied, simple, with fresh pear and lemon character; for early drinking.
styleByRegion.delle-venezie: Italian DOC covering Veneto, Friuli-Venezia Giulia and Trentino-Alto Adige. The largest premium origin for Italian-style Pinot Grigio — dry, light-bodied, simple to medium-complex, with green fruit and citrus.
styleByRegion.friuli-venezia-giulia: North-east Italy. Cooler than Veneto. Premium Pinot Grigio with more concentration and complexity than basic Italian-style Pinot Grigio.
styleByRegion.alsace: France. Cool climate, dry and sunny (rain shadow of the Vosges). 'Pinot Gris' here is full-bodied and complex — dry, off-dry or medium, with stone fruit and tropical fruit; can be made in concentrated late-harvest 'Vendanges Tardives' styles. Top sites are Alsace Grand Cru.

name: Gewurztraminer
primaryAromasNote: Aromatic variety. Floral (rose), stone fruit (peach, apricot) and tropical fruit (lychee). Distinctive perfumed character.
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age

styleByRegion.alsace: France's Alsace AOC. Cool to moderate climate. Aromatic, full-bodied wine with classic rose, lychee and stone-fruit character; low acidity. Range of styles from dry to richly sweet (e.g. late-harvest Vendanges Tardives). Top sites are designated Alsace Grand Cru.

name: Viognier
primaryAromasNote: Floral (blossom) and stone fruit (peach, apricot). Aromatic.
blendingNotes: In Côte Rôtie (Northern Rhône), small amounts of Viognier are sometimes co-fermented with Syrah to add aromatic complexity and a perfumed character.

styleByRegion.condrieu: Northern Rhône AOC just south of Côte Rôtie — the reference for varietal Viognier. Dry, medium- to full-bodied with floral (blossom) and stone-fruit (peach, apricot) character; oaked or unoaked styles.

name: Albariño
primaryAromasNote: Citrus fruit (lemon, grapefruit) and stone fruit (peach, apricot).

styleByRegion.rias-baixas: Galicia, north-west Spain on the Atlantic coast. Maritime moderate climate. The reference region for Albariño — dry, medium-bodied, with high acidity, citrus and stone fruit; typically unoaked, single varietal, for early drinking.

name: Merlot
primaryAromasNote: Fruit characteristics vary according to ripeness — less-ripe Merlot shows red fruit (strawberry, red cherry); riper Merlot shows black fruit (plum, blackberry).
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age
blendingNotes: Often blended with high-tannin varieties such as Cabernet Sauvignon — to lower tannin and acidity levels, to make wines ready to drink at an earlier age, and to add red-fruit flavours to blends.

styleByRegion.south-of-france-pgi: High-volume varietal Merlot from Pays d'Oc IGP. Warm climate gives ripe, fruit-forward, soft, simple wines for everyday drinking.
styleByRegion.bordeaux: Used at the regional Bordeaux AOC level for entry-level reds, typically blended with Cabernet Sauvignon. Soft, fruit-forward and ready to drink earlier than wines from the named appellations.
styleByRegion.saint-emilion: Right Bank Bordeaux. Merlot-dominant blends (typically with Cabernet Franc and a smaller amount of Cabernet Sauvignon). Medium- to full-bodied, plump and rounded with red and black fruit; very good to outstanding examples can age. Saint-Émilion has its own Grand Cru Classé classification.
styleByRegion.pomerol: Right Bank Bordeaux. Predominantly Merlot, with small amounts of Cabernet Franc. Concentrated, plush and full-bodied with intense plum and blackberry fruit; outstanding ageing potential.
styleByRegion.california: State-wide California GI. Warm climate produces ripe, soft, fruit-forward Merlot; oak maturation common. Available at all price points.
styleByRegion.napa-valley: Premium Merlot region. Full-bodied, ripe and concentrated; often blended Bordeaux-style with Cabernet Sauvignon. Very good to outstanding quality.
styleByRegion.sonoma: Cooler than Napa due to Pacific influence. Merlot here is medium- to full-bodied with fresher acidity than Napa.
styleByRegion.central-valley-chile: Chile's largest production zone. Reliable warmth gives ripe, soft, fruit-forward Merlot at high volume; good value.
styleByRegion.stellenbosch: South Africa's top red wine region. Warm climate produces ripe, full-bodied Merlot, often blended Bordeaux-style with Cabernet Sauvignon.
styleByRegion.margaret-river: Western Australia. Maritime climate gives structured Merlot; commonly blended with Cabernet Sauvignon in a Bordeaux style.
styleByRegion.hawkes-bay: New Zealand's warmest red wine region (North Island). Cool to moderate by world standards, so leaner and more herbaceous; usually Bordeaux-blended with Cabernet Sauvignon.

name: Cabernet Sauvignon
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age
blendingNotes: Often blended with varieties with lower tannin and acidity levels such as Merlot — to balance a wine's acidity if it is too low, and to add tannin to wine for a particular style.

styleByRegion.south-of-france-pgi: High-volume Pays d'Oc IGP Cabernet. Warm climate gives ripe, fruit-forward, simple, fruity styles for everyday drinking; usually unoaked or lightly oaked.
styleByRegion.bordeaux: Used at the regional Bordeaux AOC level for entry-level reds, typically blended with Merlot. Lighter and earlier-drinking than the named appellations.
styleByRegion.medoc: Left Bank, northern Médoc peninsula. Cabernet-dominant Bordeaux blends; good quality, generally a step below Haut-Médoc and the named communes.
styleByRegion.haut-medoc: Better-quality Left Bank zone south of the Médoc AOC. Cabernet Sauvignon-dominant Bordeaux blends; home to many Cru Bourgeois and Grand Cru Classé châteaux.
styleByRegion.pauillac: Top Haut-Médoc commune. Full-bodied, high-tannin, high-acid Cabernet-dominant blends with intense blackcurrant fruit; oak ageing adds vanilla, cedar and toast. Outstanding quality and great ageing potential. Many Grand Cru Classé châteaux.
styleByRegion.margaux: Top Haut-Médoc commune. Cabernet-dominant Bordeaux blends, full-bodied and structured with capacity for ageing. Many Grand Cru Classé châteaux.
styleByRegion.graves: Left Bank south of the city of Bordeaux. Cabernet/Merlot blends on gravelly soils; full-bodied with structured tannins.
styleByRegion.pessac-leognan: Top sub-zone of Graves. Premium Cabernet/Merlot blends with structured tannins and ageing potential; the only Bordeaux zone making both prestigious reds and dry whites.
styleByRegion.california: State-wide California GI. Warm climate produces ripe, fruit-forward, full-bodied Cabernet; oak maturation common.
styleByRegion.napa-valley: California's flagship Cabernet region. Ripe, full-bodied, with intense blackcurrant fruit; oak ageing adds vanilla and toast. Very good to outstanding quality. Sub-AVAs Oakville, Rutherford and Calistoga are particularly prized.
styleByRegion.oakville: Premium sub-AVA of Napa Valley. Concentrated, full-bodied Cabernet Sauvignon of outstanding quality.
styleByRegion.rutherford: Premium sub-AVA of Napa Valley. Concentrated, full-bodied Cabernet Sauvignon of outstanding quality.
styleByRegion.calistoga: Northernmost, warmest sub-AVA of Napa Valley. Ripe, concentrated, full-bodied Cabernet Sauvignon.
styleByRegion.sonoma: Cooler than Napa due to Pacific influence. Cabernet here is medium- to full-bodied with fresher acidity than Napa; oak ageing common.
styleByRegion.central-valley-chile: Chile's largest production zone. Reliable warmth and dry summers give ripe, fruit-forward, medium- to full-bodied Cabernet at high volume.
styleByRegion.maipo-valley: Chile's most prestigious Cabernet zone, just south of Santiago. Full-bodied with ripe blackcurrant fruit and herbaceous notes; very good to outstanding examples.
styleByRegion.colchagua-valley: Premium Cabernet zone within the Rapel sector of the Central Valley. Warm, full-bodied, ripe and concentrated.
styleByRegion.stellenbosch: South Africa's top Cabernet region. Warm climate produces ripe, full-bodied wines, often blended Bordeaux-style with Merlot.
styleByRegion.coonawarra: South Australia. Distinctive 'terra rossa' soil over limestone. Cabernet here is medium- to full-bodied with characteristic mint/eucalyptus notes alongside blackcurrant.
styleByRegion.margaret-river: Western Australia. Maritime climate gives structured, balanced Cabernet, often blended with Merlot in a Bordeaux style; a hallmark New World region for premium Cab.
styleByRegion.hawkes-bay: New Zealand's warmest red wine region (North Island). Cooler than Australia, so styles are leaner and more herbaceous; usually Bordeaux-blended with Merlot.

name: Syrah
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age
blendingNotes: In Côte Rôtie (Northern Rhône), Syrah may be co-fermented with a small amount of Viognier to add aromatic complexity. In Australia, Shiraz is sometimes blended with Cabernet Sauvignon or Grenache.

styleByRegion.south-of-france-pgi: High-volume Pays d'Oc IGP Syrah. Warm climate gives ripe, fruit-forward, peppery, medium- to full-bodied wines for everyday drinking.
styleByRegion.northern-rhone: Northern part of the Rhône Valley. Syrah is the only black grape used in red AOC wines. Moderate climate gives medium- to full-bodied Syrah with black fruit and pepper; oak maturation common.
styleByRegion.crozes-hermitage: Largest Northern Rhône AOC, surrounding the Hermitage hill. Medium- to full-bodied Syrah with black fruit and pepper; good quality and accessibly priced.
styleByRegion.hermitage: Tiny, prestigious AOC on a steep hill above Tain l'Hermitage. Concentrated, full-bodied Syrah of outstanding quality with great ageing potential. Intense black fruit and spice, developing tertiary leather, meat and earth notes with age.
styleByRegion.cote-rotie: Northernmost Northern Rhône AOC ('roasted slope'). Steep south-facing terraced vineyards. Syrah may be co-fermented with a small amount of Viognier, giving an aromatic, perfumed style. Concentrated, structured, outstanding ageing potential.
styleByRegion.south-eastern-australia: Multi-state catch-all GI covering Victoria, NSW, Tasmania and parts of South Australia and Queensland. Used for high-volume varietal Shiraz blended from across regions; ripe, soft, fruit-forward.
styleByRegion.barossa-valley: South Australia. The icon for warm-climate Shiraz. Full-bodied, ripe, with intense black fruit and vanilla and toast from oak; high alcohol; outstanding examples can age.
styleByRegion.hunter-valley: New South Wales. Warm but humid and often cloudy, which moderates ripeness. Distinctive medium-bodied Shiraz with a characteristic earthy, leathery character. Also famous for long-lived dry Sémillon.

name: Gamay
primaryAromasNote: Red fruit (raspberry, red cherry, red plum). Carbonic maceration is widely used in Beaujolais, adding distinctive banana and candy aromas.
winemaking: Carbonic maceration commonly used (adds banana and candy aromas)

styleByRegion.beaujolais: Granite-soiled region just south of Burgundy. Light-bodied, high-acid, low-tannin Gamay; carbonic maceration is widely used, adding banana and candy notes. Three-tier hierarchy: Beaujolais AOC, Beaujolais Villages AOC, ten named Crus.
styleByRegion.beaujolais-villages: Mid-tier Beaujolais AOC from a defined group of villages in the northern half of the region. More structured and concentrated than basic Beaujolais.
styleByRegion.fleurie: One of the ten Beaujolais Crus (top tier). Elegant, perfumed Gamay; medium-bodied with red fruit and floral character.

name: Grenache
primaryAromasNote: Red fruit (strawberry, red plum, red cherry) and spice (white pepper, liquorice). High sugar levels give high alcohol.
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age
blendingNotes: Commonly blended with Syrah and Mourvèdre in the Southern Rhône (and as 'GSM' in Australia), and with Carignan/Cariñena in Spain (especially Priorat). Used for both red and rosé.

styleByRegion.southern-rhone: Southern part of the Rhône Valley. Warm climate. Grenache is the dominant black grape, typically blended with Syrah and Mourvèdre.
styleByRegion.cotes-du-rhone: Largest Rhône AOC. Grenache-based blends; medium- to full-bodied, ripe red fruit and white pepper; usually unoaked or lightly oaked. Accessibly priced.
styleByRegion.cotes-du-rhone-villages: Higher-quality tier above Côtes du Rhône AOC, from a defined group of villages. More concentrated Grenache-based blends than basic Côtes du Rhône.
styleByRegion.chateauneuf-du-pape: Top Southern Rhône AOC. Famous for galets (large round stones) in the vineyards. Grenache-dominant blends with up to 13 permitted varieties; full-bodied, ripe, complex with red fruit, spice and earthy character; outstanding ageing potential.
styleByRegion.minervois: Languedoc-Roussillon AOC. Warm Mediterranean climate. Grenache-based blends, similar in style to the Southern Rhône but typically simpler and more affordable.
styleByRegion.rioja: Spain's most famous wine region. Garnacha is blended with Tempranillo to add body, ripe red fruit and softness.
styleByRegion.navarra: Spanish region adjacent to Rioja. Garnacha is widely planted; used for rosé (rosado) and as a blending component.
styleByRegion.priorat: Mountainous Catalan DOCa. Distinctive slate soils ('llicorella'). Old-vine Garnacha (often blended with Cariñena) producing concentrated, full-bodied, age-worthy reds of outstanding quality.
styleByRegion.barossa-valley: South Australia. Old-vine Grenache produces concentrated, full-bodied wines with ripe red fruit and white pepper; often blended with Shiraz and Mourvèdre (GSM).
styleByRegion.mclaren-vale: South Australia. Warm Mediterranean climate. Old-vine Grenache gives ripe, full-bodied wines with red fruit and white pepper; commonly blended GSM.

name: Carmenère
primaryAromasNote: Herbaceous (green pepper), herbal (eucalyptus) and black fruit (blackberry).
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age

styleByRegion.central-valley-chile: Chile's largest production zone. Carmenère is Chile's signature black grape — full-bodied, ripe blackberry with characteristic herbaceous (green pepper) and herbal (eucalyptus) notes; often oak-aged. Long mistaken for Merlot before being identified as a distinct variety in the 1990s.

name: Malbec
primaryAromasNote: Black fruit (blackberry, black plum).
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age

styleByRegion.mendoza: Argentina's flagship region, in the rain shadow of the Andes. High-altitude, warm, dry. Full-bodied Malbec with intense ripe blackberry and black plum, high tannin, often oak-aged.

name: Pinotage
primaryAromasNote: Red fruit (strawberry, raspberry, red cherry).
agingNote: Takes on strong flavours from oak (coffee, chocolate, smoke); can age
winemaking: Used in 'Cape Blend' wines (with international varieties)

styleByRegion.western-cape: South Africa's distinctive grape — a cross of Pinot Noir × Cinsaut bred in 1925. Full-bodied with red fruit; takes on strong oak-derived coffee, chocolate and smoke flavours when matured. Used in 'Cape Blend' wines together with international varieties (e.g. Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Shiraz).

name: Tempranillo
primaryAromasNote: Red fruit (strawberry, red cherry) and black fruit (blackberry, black plum).
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age
blendingNotes: In Rioja, traditionally blended with Garnacha (for body) and small amounts of Mazuelo and Graciano. In Catalunya, often blended with international varieties (Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot).

styleByRegion.rioja: Spain's most famous wine region. Tempranillo-dominant, blended with Garnacha. Strict Spanish ageing classifications (Joven, Crianza, Reserva, Gran Reserva) on the label indicate increasing oak and bottle ageing — older wines develop characteristic dried fruit, leather and mushroom notes.
styleByRegion.ribera-del-duero: High-altitude Spanish region on the Duero river. Tempranillo (locally called Tinto Fino or Tinta del País). Cool nights despite warm days; concentrated, full-bodied, structured wines with significant ageing potential.
styleByRegion.catalunya: Broad Spanish DO covering all of Catalonia (Priorat is a separate, prestigious DOCa within). Tempranillo here is typically blended with international varieties (Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot).

name: Cortese
primaryAromasNote: Floral (blossom), green fruit (apple, pear) and citrus (lemon).

styleByRegion.gavi: Piemonte DOCG (Gavi or Gavi di Gavi). Cortese-based dry white. Light-bodied, unoaked, with high acidity, floral and citrus character; typically for early drinking.

name: Garganega
primaryAromasNote: Green fruit (apple, pear), citrus (lemon) and stone fruit (peach).
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age

styleByRegion.soave: Veneto DOC, near Verona. Garganega-based dry whites; medium-bodied, unoaked, with high acidity and green fruit, citrus and stone fruit. Mostly for early drinking. Sweet style is Recioto di Soave (DOCG).
styleByRegion.soave-classico: Hilly heart of the Soave zone (the historic 'classico' core). Same Garganega-based whites but generally higher quality and more concentrated; very good or outstanding examples can age, developing honey and almond notes.

name: Verdicchio
primaryAromasNote: Green fruit (apple, pear), citrus (lemon) and herbal (fennel).
agingNote: Outstanding examples can age

styleByRegion.verdicchio-dei-castelli-di-jesi: Marche, central Italy. Dry, medium-bodied, unoaked Verdicchio with high acidity and characteristic green fruit, lemon and herbal (fennel) notes. Typically for early drinking; outstanding examples can age, developing honey and nuts.

name: Fiano
primaryAromasNote: Stone fruit (peach) and tropical fruit (melon, mango).
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age

styleByRegion.fiano-di-avellino: Campania DOCG, southern Italy. Dry, medium- to full-bodied Fiano with stone and tropical fruit; can be oaked or aged on lees. Very good or outstanding examples can age, developing honey notes.

name: Nebbiolo
primaryAromasNote: Red fruit (red cherry, red plum), floral (rose, violet) and herbal (dried herbs).
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age

styleByRegion.barolo: Top Piemonte DOCG in the Langhe hills. Full-bodied, high-tannin, high-acid Nebbiolo of outstanding quality; oak maturation common; ages exceptionally well, developing tertiary mushroom, tobacco and leather notes.
styleByRegion.barbaresco: Top Piemonte DOCG, just north-east of Barolo. Full-bodied Nebbiolo, often slightly more elegant and earlier-drinking than Barolo; outstanding ageing potential.

name: Barbera
primaryAromasNote: Red fruit (red cherry, red plum) with black pepper spice.
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age

styleByRegion.barbera-d-asti: Piemonte DOCG. Single varietal Barbera with high acidity, low to medium tannin, red fruit and black pepper spice. Oaked or unoaked styles; can be for early drinking, very good examples can age.

name: Corvina
primaryAromasNote: Red fruit (red cherry, red plum).
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age (especially Amarone)
winemaking: Appassimento method sometimes used (grapes dried before fermentation)
blendingNotes: Corvina is the principal grape, blended with local varieties such as Rondinella and Molinara. Appassimento method (drying grapes for several months) is used to make Amarone (full-bodied dry red) and Recioto (sweet red).

styleByRegion.valpolicella: Veneto DOC, east of Lake Garda. Corvina-based blend with local varieties. From fresh grapes — light- to medium-bodied, fresh, with red cherry and red plum; usually for early drinking. Top wines (Amarone, Recioto) are made from dried grapes by the appassimento method.
styleByRegion.valpolicella-classico: Hilly heart of the Valpolicella zone (the historic 'classico' core). Same Corvina-based blend, generally higher quality and more concentrated than basic Valpolicella.

name: Sangiovese
primaryAromasNote: Red fruit (red cherry, red plum) and herbal (dried herbs).
agingNote: Very good or outstanding examples can age
winemaking: Often blended with local and international varieties
blendingNotes: Often blended with local Tuscan varieties (Canaiolo, Colorino) and increasingly with international varieties (Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot). Brunello di Montalcino is made from 100% Sangiovese.

styleByRegion.chianti: Tuscany DOCG. Sangiovese-dominant blends with local and international varieties. Medium- to full-bodied, often oaked, with high acidity, high tannin and red fruit/dried herbs. Wide quality range; can be for early drinking.
styleByRegion.chianti-classico: Hilly heart of the Chianti zone (the historic 'classico' core), now its own DOCG. Higher-quality Sangiovese-based wines than basic Chianti; very good to outstanding examples can age. Riserva indicates extended ageing.
styleByRegion.brunello-di-montalcino: Top Tuscany DOCG, made from 100% Sangiovese (locally called Brunello). Concentrated, full-bodied, high-tannin wines with extensive oak ageing requirements; outstanding ageing potential.

name: Montepulciano
primaryAromasNote: Black fruit (plum, blackberry). [Verify against textbook chapter 23 — not covered in the L2E06 lesson PDF.]

styleByRegion.montepulciano-d-abruzzo: Abruzzo DOC, central Italy on the Adriatic coast. Medium- to full-bodied red from the Montepulciano grape (NOT to be confused with Tuscany's Vino Nobile di Montepulciano, which is Sangiovese-based). Soft, approachable, with black fruit; often good value.

name: Meunier
primaryAromasNote: One of the three permitted grapes in Champagne, alongside Chardonnay and Pinot Noir. Buds late and ripens early — useful in Champagne's marginal climate. Contributes fruitiness and approachability to non-vintage blends.
winemaking: Used as base wine for Traditional Method sparkling
Blended with Chardonnay and Pinot Noir
blendingNotes: Almost always blended in Champagne with Chardonnay (acidity, finesse) and Pinot Noir (body, structure).

styleByRegion.champagne: One of the three permitted Champagne grapes. Adds fruity, approachable character to non-vintage blends. Rarely seen as a single varietal.

name: Glera
primaryAromasNote: Glera's character is fresh apple and pear with floral notes; the Tank Method (Charmat) preserves these primary fruit aromas rather than developing autolytic complexity.
winemaking: Tank Method (second fermentation in pressurised tank)
Bottled under pressure to preserve fresh fruit
Made for early drinking — does not benefit from ageing

styleByRegion.prosecco: The grape of Prosecco DOC (Veneto/Friuli). Light-bodied sparkling, off-dry to dry, with apple and pear. Charmat Method preserves primary fruit. Made for early drinking.

name: Muscat
alternateName: Moscato / Muscat Blanc à Petits Grains
primaryAromasNote: One of the few grapes whose wine smells distinctly like fresh grapes. Highly aromatic with floral (rose, orange blossom) and fresh-grape character.
winemaking: Asti DOCG: Tank Method, fermentation stopped early — sweet, low-alcohol, lightly sparkling
Moscato d'Asti: lightly sparkling (frizzante), even lower alcohol
Also made as fortified sweet wine (e.g. Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise)

styleByRegion.asti: Piedmont DOCG. Sweet, lightly sparkling, low-alcohol. Tank Method with fermentation stopped early to retain natural grape sugar. Intensely grapey and floral; balanced by high acidity. Made for early drinking.
